verses.commentary: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, discovered in this matter? Paul turns to Abraham, the foundational figure of Jewish identity. If anyone could be justified by works, Abraham could. His example will prove justification by faith.

If Abraham was justified by works, he had something to boast about, but not before God. Human boasting might exist among men, but not in God's presence. No one can boast before God because all stand guilty.

What does Scripture say? Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness (Genesis 15:6). Credited (logizomai, λογίζομαι) is an accounting term: righteousness is reckoned, imputed, counted to Abraham's account. Faith, not works, was the ground of his justification.
To the one who works, wages are not credited as a gift but as an obligation. Works create debt; the employer owes the worker. This is the principle of merit. But salvation is not by merit.

However, to the one who does not work but trusts God who justifies the ungodly, their faith is credited as righteousness. This is shocking: God justifies the ungodly. Not the godly but the ungodly. Faith, not works, is credited as righteousness. Salvation is by trust, not effort.

David says the same thing when he speaks of the blessedness of the one to whom God credits righteousness apart from works. David, like Abraham, understood justification by faith. Psalm 32 describes the blessing of forgiveness apart from works.

Blessed are those whose transgressions are forgiven, whose sins are covered (Psalm 32:1). Forgiveness is a covering of sin, not the absence of sin. God deals with sin by covering it through atonement.

Blessed is the one whose sin the Lord will never count against them (Psalm 32:2). Non-imputation of sin: God does not charge sin to the believer's account. This is the negative side of justification (sin not counted). The positive side is righteousness credited.

Is this blessedness only for the circumcised, or also for the uncircumcised? We have been saying that Abraham's faith was credited to him as righteousness. Paul asks: Does justification by faith apply only to Jews or also to Gentiles? Abraham's example will answer this.

Under what circumstances was it credited? Was it after he was circumcised, or before? It was not after, but before! Timing is crucial. Abraham was justified in Genesis 15, before he was circumcised in Genesis 17. Faith preceded the covenant sign.

He received circumcision as a sign, a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while still uncircumcised. Circumcision confirmed what already existed. It was a sign (pointing to reality) and a seal (authenticating reality), but not the cause of righteousness. So then, he is the father of all who believe but have not been circumcised, in order that righteousness might be credited to them. Abraham is the father of believing Gentiles. Faith, not circumcision, makes one Abraham's child.

And he is then also the father of the circumcised who not only are circumcised but who also follow in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had before he was circumcised. True children of Abraham have his faith, not just his physical descent. Circumcision without faith does not make one Abraham's child.

It was not through the law that Abraham and his offspring received the promise that he would be heir of the world, but through the righteousness that comes by faith. The promise predates the law (which came 430 years later, Galatians 3:17). Law-keeping was not the condition of the promise; faith was.

For if those who depend on the law are heirs, faith means nothing and the promise is worthless. If law-keeping is required, the promise is nullified. Law and promise are incompatible as bases of inheritance. Promise implies grace; law implies works.

Because the law brings wrath. And where there is no law there is no transgression. The law exposes sin and condemns it. Transgression (parabasis, παράβασις) is breaking a known command. Law makes sin explicit and culpable. Law produces wrath, not salvation.

Therefore, the promise comes by faith, so that it may be by grace and may be guaranteed to all Abraham's offspring, not only to those who are of the law but also to those who have the faith of Abraham. He is the father of us all. Grace is the ground; faith is the means. This guarantees the promise to all who believe, Jew and Gentile. Works would limit the promise; faith opens it to all.

As it is written: I have made you a father of many nations (Genesis 17:5). Abraham is the father in the sight of God, in whom he believed, the God who gives life to the dead and calls into being things that were not. God's creative power is the basis of Abraham's faith. God creates life from death (Isaac from a dead womb) and calls things into existence by His word.

Against all hope, Abraham in hope believed and so became the father of many nations, just as it had been said to him, So shall your offspring be (Genesis 15:5). Abraham's faith was against human hope but grounded in divine promise. He believed God's word despite impossible circumstances.

Without weakening in his faith, he faced the fact that his body was as good as dead (since he was about a hundred years old) and that Sarah's womb was also dead. Abraham did not deny reality; he faced it. Yet his faith did not weaken. True faith acknowledges difficulty but trusts God's power.

Yet he did not waver through unbelief regarding the promise of God, but was strengthened in his faith and gave glory to God. Unwavering faith does not mean absence of struggle but perseverance despite difficulty. Faith glorifies God by trusting His word above circumstances.

Being fully persuaded that God had power to do what He had promised. Faith rests on God's power and faithfulness. Full persuasion (plērophoreō, πληροφορέω) means complete conviction. Abraham's faith was in God's ability and integrity.
This is why it was credited to him as righteousness. Faith in God's promise was counted as righteousness. This is the climax: Abraham's faith, not his works, justified him.
The words it was credited to him were written not for him alone. Abraham's justification by faith is not just historical; it is paradigmatic. His experience establishes the pattern for all believers.

But also for us, to whom God will credit righteousness, for us who believe in Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. We are justified the same way Abraham was: by faith. The object of faith is different (Jesus, not Isaac), but the principle is the same. God's power to raise the dead is the ground of our faith.

He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification. Christ's death dealt with sin (atonement). His resurrection secured justification (vindication). Both are necessary. Death pays the penalty; resurrection declares acceptance. Our justification is grounded in Christ's finished work.
